---
title: "Prediction Background: Atlantic Cyclone before July 15, 2026"
---

# The Prediction Question

**Will at least one named tropical cyclone form in the Atlantic Ocean before July 15, 2026?**

*Resolution:* The question resolves YES if the National Hurricane Center (NHC) officially designates at least one tropical or subtropical storm (receiving a name) in the Atlantic basin — including the Gulf of Mexico and Caribbean — with formation date before July 15, 2026. It resolves NO if the season remains entirely quiet through July 14.

---

# Historical Climatology (Base Rate)

## When does the first storm typically form?

- The **climatological median date** for the first Atlantic named storm is around **June 20**.
- This means that in roughly half of all seasons, a named storm forms before June 20 — more than three weeks before the July 15 threshold.
- Extending to July 15 captures the large majority of seasons historically.

## Recent trends: earlier seasons

Atlantic hurricane seasons have started earlier over recent decades:

- From **2015 through 2024**, only **two** of ten seasons had no named storm before June 1.
- Five of the past twelve seasons had a storm form as early as **May**.
- A 2022 study in *Nature Communications* found that the threshold date for first Atlantic cyclone activity has shifted **earlier by more than five days per decade** since 1979 — a statistically significant trend attributed partly to warming sea surface temperatures.

## June–July climatology

- **June:** Activity occurs in roughly half of all seasons. The record (as of 2024) is three named storms in a single June. June storms typically form close to land — Gulf of Mexico or southeastern U.S. coast.
- **July:** More than half of seasons feature at least some July activity. Since 1851, approximately 105 named storms have formed in July.

**Bottom line on base rate:** Roughly **85–90% of seasons** produce at least one named storm before July 15, based on the historical record and recent trends toward earlier seasons.

---

# 2026 Season Forecasts

## Official forecasts (as of late May 2026)

| Forecaster | Named Storms | Hurricanes | Major Hurricanes |
|---|---|---|---|
| NOAA (May 21) | 8–14 | 3–6 | 1–3 |
| CSU (April) | 13 | 6 | 2 |
| Historical average | ~14 | ~7 | ~3 |

NOAA assigns a **55% probability of a below-normal season**, 35% near-normal, 10% above-normal.

## El Niño: the dominant suppressing factor

The primary reason for the below-normal forecast is a **developing El Niño**, potentially among the strongest on record:

- El Niño increases **vertical wind shear** over the tropical Atlantic — winds at upper levels blow in a different direction than at lower levels, literally tearing apart developing thunderstorm clusters that would otherwise organize into tropical storms.
- The 2026 wind shear is projected to be the **second-highest since 1981**, surpassed only by 2015.
- CSU describes El Niño as likely to be the **dominant suppressing force** for the 2026 season.

## Partially offsetting factors

- **Atlantic sea surface temperatures** (SSTs) remain near or slightly above average — warmer water provides thermodynamic fuel for storm development even under high shear.
- El Niño suppresses overall *season totals*, but **early-season storms** (May–June) often form before El Niño's influence fully sets in and in regions (Gulf of Mexico, western Caribbean) less exposed to shear.

## Season status as of May 26, 2026

No named storms have formed yet. The official Atlantic hurricane season begins June 1.

---

# Comparable Historical Seasons

El Niño years provide the best analogues for 2026. Key cases:

| Year | El Niño? | Season total | First storm | Before July 15? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2015 | Strong | 11 named | Ana (Apr 19) | **YES** |
| 2014 | Neutral/weak | 8 named | Arthur (Jul 1) | **YES** |
| 2013 | Neutral | 2 named | Andrea (Jun 5) | **YES** |
| 2012 | Neutral | 19 named | Alberto (May 19) | **YES** |
| 2009 | Moderate El Niño | 9 named | Ana (Aug 15) | **NO** |
| 2006 | Weak El Niño | 10 named | Alberto (Jun 10) | **YES** |

**Key observation:** 2009 is the clearest modern precedent for a season in which the first named storm did not form until mid-August — during a moderate El Niño year with suppressed activity. The 2026 El Niño is expected to be considerably stronger.

---

# Sources

- [NOAA 2026 Atlantic Hurricane Season Forecast](https://www.noaa.gov/news-release/noaa-predicts-below-normal-2026-atlantic-hurricane-season)
- [NHC Tropical Cyclone Climatology](https://www.nhc.noaa.gov/climo/)
- [CSU 2026 Forecast — Fox Weather](https://www.foxweather.com/weather-news/2026-hurricane-season-colorado-state-university-forecast-el-nino)
- [El Niño and 2026 Hurricane Season — CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/el-nino-2026-hurricane-season-atlantic-pacific/)
- [Earlier onset of Atlantic hurricane season — Nature Communications (2022)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-022-31821-3)
- [When the first storm Arthur may form in 2026 — Weather.com](https://weather.com/2026/05/22/storms/hurricane/when-the-atlantic-hurricane-seasons-first-storm-arthur-may-form)
